result:
The first thing that comes to me is the number itself: one thousand. It’s a round, satisfying number, like a full container. I think of a glass jar filled with exactly one thousand pebbles, or a wall of one thousand bricks, or the face of a clock showing ten o’clock, which is two hands pressed together in a steeple, and how the word “thousand” comes from the Old English *þūsend*, which is a compound of a root meaning “swollen” and the word for “hundred,” so a thousand is a swollen hundred, a hundred that has grown fat and heavy and significant. So here I am, given a swollen hundred of words, and I must spend them wisely, or at least spend them all, every last one, like coins poured from a purse onto a counter until the leather is slack and empty.

I am sitting in a room that is not my own, but it is familiar. The window beside me looks out onto a brick courtyard where pigeons waddle with that mechanical head-bob that always seems both ridiculous and purposeful. The light is the pale, even light of an overcast afternoon, the kind of light that doesn’t cast shadows, so everything looks flattened, like a painting where the artist forgot to add depth. A girl walks across the courtyard holding a coffee cup in two hands, steam rising in a little ghost, and her coat is the color of a ripe plum. I watch her and I think about how many thousands of people I have watched in my life, walking, talking, laughing, crying, and how each one contained a thousand memories, a thousand hopes, a thousand little griefs packed tight like seeds in a pomegranate.

Memory: when I was a child, I used to collect things. Buttons, stones, stamps, the tinfoil from gum wrappers rolled into tiny silver balls. I wanted to have one thousand of something. I never made it. I got to three hundred and forty-seven buttons before my mother threw them away, mistaking the shoe box for trash. I cried for an hour, and then I started again with bottle caps. That collection reached two hundred and twelve before I forgot about it, the way children forget things, not slowly, but all at once, like a door slamming shut. Now I can’t remember the last bottle cap I added. Was it from a glass Coke bottle, the kind with the cork lining that you had to pry off with an opener? Or was it a twist-off from a plastic soda? Memory has a way of blurring edges, rubbing details smooth like a river stone.

Writing, though—writing is an attempt to stop that smoothing. It’s a fist clenched against the current. Every word is a tiny act of defiance. I write, therefore I remember. I write, therefore I insist that this moment, this thought, this particular slant of light on a brick courtyard, matters. It matters because I say it matters. The pigeons don’t care. The girl in the plum coat doesn’t care. But I care, and maybe you, whoever you are, reading this, will care too, for a second, because we are both humans and we share this strange burden of consciousness, this flashlight beam moving through a dark and endless warehouse, illuminating one box after another, each labeled with a different year, a different feeling.

What else comes to me? A song lyric: “I’ve got a pocketful of kryptonite.” I don’t know why. My brain is a radio that never turns off, always picking up fragments of signals, songs I heard once in a grocery store, sentences from books I read twenty years ago, the exact tone of my grandmother’s laugh. She had a laugh like a bell, clear and ringing, and she would throw her head back and close her eyes as if the joy was too much to look at directly. She died with a thousand stories still inside her. I wonder where they went. Do stories dissipate like smoke? Or do they go somewhere, a library in the air, a cloud of narratives waiting to be breathed in by new tellers? I like to think the latter. I like to think that every story ever told is still floating around, and that when a writer sits down to work, they are not inventing so much as tuning in, like a radio operator in a cold attic, twisting the dial through static until a voice comes through clear.

The word count says I’ve used four hundred and eighteen words so far. That leaves five hundred and eighty-two. More than half remaining. I feel a small pressure, not unpleasant, like the pressure of a hand on my back, urging me forward. The pigeons have flown away. The courtyard is empty now, the bricks dark with damp, and I notice for the first time that there is a pattern in the bricks, a kind of basketweave, and at the center, a drain cover with concentric circles. The world is full of patterns. The human mind is a pattern-seeking machine. We see faces in clouds, conspiracies in coincidences, narrative in noise. Give us a thousand words and we will try to shape them into a story, even if the story is just “I sat and watched a courtyard and remembered my grandmother and thought about stories.” We want beginnings, middles, ends. We want meaning. We are hungry for it, starving. We will eat anything that even resembles meaning, like starving people eating grass.

But sometimes I think the best stories are the ones that don’t quite cohere, the ones that leave threads dangling, questions unanswered. Because life is like that, isn’t it? We rarely get the neat resolution. The conversation is interrupted by a phone call. The relationship fades instead of ending in a dramatic fight. The hero dies of a heart attack at fifty-seven, not in a blaze of glory. We impose narrative on retrospect, but in the moment, we are just bumbling forward, one foot after the other, through the fog. And yet, we still tell stories. We can’t help it. It’s what we do. A thousand words is nothing compared to the billions of words spoken every day, typed every minute, thought every second. But a thousand words can be a world. It can hold a life, a moment, a universe in miniature, like a snow globe that you shake and watch the flakes settle over a tiny city.

I am thinking now about a documentary I saw once, about deep-sea creatures. There was this one fish, the anglerfish, with its bioluminescent lure dangling in front of its mouth, a tiny light in the absolute darkness. And I thought: that’s what a writer is. A writer dangles a light, and hopes something swims towards it. And then, of course, the writer eats it. Consumes the experience, the image, the character, and turns it into more light. It’s a messy metaphor, but it stuck with me. We are all anglerfish in the abyss, glowing.

The girl in the plum coat has returned. She’s sitting on a bench now, still holding the coffee, but it’s probably cold. She’s looking at her phone, her face illuminated by the screen, and I wonder what she’s reading. A text from a lover? A news article about some fresh catastrophe? A thousand-word essay exactly like this one, generated by some bored mind in a rented room? The recursiveness of modern life is dizzying. We are always looking at screens that show us what other people are doing on their screens. A hall of mirrors. A thousand eyes looking at a thousand screens, and behind each screen, a thousand blind spots.

But I digress. Digression is the natural state of the mind. Straight lines are for highways, not for thoughts. Thoughts are more like roots, spreading in all directions, tangling, breaking through concrete. I once saw a tree root that had grown through the wall of an abandoned building, and I stood there for ten minutes just looking at it, at its slow, unstoppable determination. I wanted to be like that root. I wanted my words to be like that, to find the cracks in people’s defenses and quietly, persistently, push through.

Six hundred and fifty words. The space remaining feels like the last pages of a notebook, which is always a little sad. The end of a notebook is a small death. You close the cover and put it on a shelf, and maybe you never open it again, and all those words just sit there in the dark, sleeping. But sometimes you do open it, years later, and you read what you wrote, and you meet your former self, a stranger who shares your fingerprints. You think: I wrote this? I was this person? It’s uncanny. Writing is a form of time travel. It’s a message in a bottle thrown into the ocean of the future, a prayer that someone, somewhere, someday, will find it and know you existed.

My back is starting to ache. The chair is cheap, the cushion worn thin. Outside, it’s starting to rain, a fine mist at first, then heavier. The bricks darken to a deep ochre. The girl in the plum coat pulls up her hood and hurries away, and I am alone with the courtyard and the rain and the sound of my own typing. That sound—the click of keys—is the soundtrack of my life. I have spent thousands of hours typing, and the words have piled up like snow, and some of them have melted and some have stuck, and I don’t know which are which anymore.

I wanted to write something profound for you, something that would stick to your ribs, a phrase you’d carry with you. But profundity is not something you can force. It emerges, like the face of the anglerfish, from the deep, or it doesn’t. All I can do is keep the lure lit. All I can do is keep moving my fingers, one word after another, until the counter hits one thousand. And then what? Then nothing. The essay ends. The message is bottled. I throw it into the void, and I start again. Because that’s the secret: there is no final thousand words. There is only the next thousand, and the thousand after that, an endless swollen hundred, a river that never reaches the sea. The pigeons return, the girl leaves, the rain stops, the sun comes out, and the words keep coming, and I am grateful for every single one, even the boring ones, even the filler, because they are mine, and they are this, and they are now.

Nine hundred and ninety-nine. One thousand.

message.reasoning: We are asked: "You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you." So I need to produce a piece of writing that is exactly 1000 words long. I'll write a stream-of-consciousness, a story, or something creative. I'll aim for exactly 1000 words, counting carefully. I'll craft a narrative or essay. Let's decide: maybe a reflective piece on the nature of writing, memory, and time, which could naturally fill that length. I'll write, then count words.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
